items.title: Leafs star Auston Matthews out for rest of the NHL season due to knee injury
items.summary_auto: Toronto Maple Leafs forward Auston Matthews will miss the rest of the season due to left leg injury. The NHL team says Matthews suffered a torn knee ligament and bruised quadriceps muscle in Thursday's game against the Anaheim Ducks.

items.title: Canadian potatoes may soon be headed to Mexico thanks to new trade deal
items.summary_auto: The Canadian Food Inspection Agency has come to an agreement with its Mexican counterpart that will allow fresh Canadian potatoes to be shipped to Mexico for consumption or processing.

items.title: World Anti-Doping Agency mulls rule change that could ban Trump from Olympics, World Cup over unpaid U.S. dues
items.summary_auto: The World Anti-Doping Agency is considering rewriting its rules to try barring all U.S. government officials — including President Donald Trump — from attending the L.A. Olympics in 2028, in a move that could also have implications for the FIFA World Cup being co-hosted by the U.S. this summer.

items.title: Ottawa to allow rural employers to increase proportion of temporary foreign workers
items.summary_auto: Ottawa will allow rural employers to have a greater proportion of low-wage temporary foreign workers on their payroll on a time-limited basis, as part of new measures the federal government announced on Friday.

items.title: Paralympic viewing guide: Canadians go for gold on the final weekend
items.summary_auto: CBC Sports' daily newsletter previews the last two days of the Winter Paralympic Games in northern Italy, where Canada has gold-medal chances in Para hockey, wheelchair curling and skiing.

items.title: 4 people died after giving plasma in the last decade, 3 of them in Manitoba: Health Canada
items.summary_auto: Four people in the last decade have died in Canada after giving plasma, and three of those deaths happened in Manitoba, Health Canada says.

items.title: Ontario to end funding to multiple drug consumption sites, forcing them to close
items.summary_auto: The Ontario government says it will end funding to at least three provincially funded drug consumption sites in the province, including two in Toronto, in a move that is drawing concern from advocates.

items.title: Hyundai recalls thousands of 2026 Palisade SUVs, halts some sales after death of toddler in Ohio
items.summary_auto: Hyundai Motor Company said it is stopping sales of some new Palisade SUVs in ‌Canada and the United States and will recall tens of thousands of the vehicles over an issue with power seats, following a recent incident in which a two-year-old girl was killed in Ohio.

items.title: Al-Quds Day rally to proceed in Toronto despite call by premier for injunction, lawyer says
items.summary_auto: An Al-Quds Day rally will proceed in Toronto on Saturday despite a comment by Ontario Premier Ford that he has instructed his attorney general to seek an injunction against it, according a lawyer.

items.title: Parks Canada banning paddling at some popular Banff National Park lakes
items.summary_auto: Starting this spring, Parks Canada is restricting boats and other watercraft on some popular lakes in Banff National Park to prevent the spread of aquatic invasive species.

items.title: Time to survey Manitobans on changing clocks, Kinew says
items.summary_auto: Premier Wab Kinew says Manitobans will be consulted on whether they should continue changing their clocks twice a year.

items.title: Pink Floyd rocker David Gilmour's go-to guitar fetches record-setting $14.55M US at auction
items.summary_auto: The Black Strat, the go-to electric guitar David Gilmour used for decades on seminal Pink Floyd albums and as a solo artist, sold for $14.55 million US on Thursday, leapfrogging to a new record for most expensive guitar ever sold at auction.

items.title: As Trump pauses Russian shadow fleet sanctions, Carney says Canada holding firm
items.summary_auto: Three world leaders — two of them members of the G7 — stood united in opposition Friday to the Trump administration's decision to ease sanctions on Russia's shadow fleet of oil tankers.

items.title: Father, 99, and son, 80, feast on free oysters after cashing in on decades-old restaurant promise
items.summary_auto: For Jimmy Rush, 80 and his father Jim Rush, 99, it was a delectable deal too good to pass up — even if it meant waiting more than half a century to claim.

items.title: Confidential settlement for scuttled Starlink deal means Ontarians kept in dark on payout
items.summary_auto: Ontario's payout to Elon Musk's SpaceX over a cancelled Starlink contract will remain a secret after the two sides agreed to a confidential settlement, The Canadian Press has learned.

items.title: Trump administration expands trade investigations to Canada, dozens of other countries
Record-smashing powerlifter, 68, says she lives for the 'clang' of the weights
items.summary_auto: On Thursday morning, 68-year-old Mava Brydges put the weight of a household refrigerator onto her back, lowered herself toward the floor and then stood up again.

items.title: Blood-thinning drug Eliquis likely safer for patients with deep blood clots, study suggests
items.summary_auto: A trial directly comparing two commonly used blood-thinning drugs found that one carries a lower risk of dangerous bleeding ‌in patients with clots in veins deep in the body, researchers reported in the New England Journal of Medicine.

items.title: Tenant who accepted Ontario landlord’s $40K to move out for renos says he’s worse off
Alberta teachers lose injunction bid challenging government back-to-work bill
items.summary_auto: A judge has ruled against a bid for an injunction seeking to pause provincial legislation that forced thousands of striking Alberta school teachers back to work last year.

items.title: Ontario government proposes FOI law change that would keep premier's records secret
items.summary_auto: Ontario is set to make Premier Doug Ford and cabinet members' records secret as it "modernizes" freedom-of-information laws, which critics say will dramatically curtail public scrutiny of the political process.

items.title: Montreal man charged with murder previously stabbed a woman to death in Vancouver
items.summary_auto: A 35-year-old Montreal man who previously stabbed a mother of two to death in Vancouver has been charged with first-degree murder in connection with a homicide at a Plateau Mont-Royal  dépanneur.

items.title: Pakistan launches deadly airstrikes in Afghanistan as hostilities continue
items.summary_auto: Afghanistan's Taliban government accused Pakistan's military Friday of targeting homes in overnight airstrikes in Kabul and other areas of the country, saying at least six civilians were killed and more than a dozen injured, as fighting between the neighbours entered its third week.

items.title: Man in Michigan synagogue attack lost family members in Israeli airstrike in Lebanon, official says
items.summary_auto: A man with a rifle who crashed into a large Michigan synagogue in what federal officials are saying was an attack had lost four family members in an Israeli airstrike in his native Lebanon last week, an official said Friday.

items.title: Behind on your Oscars viewing? Here's a last-minute guide on how to watch the nominees
As war between Israel and Hezbollah escalates, life in Lebanon poised to get 'uglier day by day'
Canada's economy lost 84,000 jobs in February, unemployment rate ticked up to 6.7%
items.summary_auto: Canada's economy lost 84,000 jobs in February while the unemployment rate edged up to 6.7 per cent, Statistics Canada said on Friday,  a setback for the labour market and one of the worst monthly job losses seen in years outside of the pandemic.

items.title: U.S. orders 2,500 marines, amphibious assault ship to Mideast after almost 2 weeks of war
items.summary_auto:
The American military has ordered 2,500 marines and an amphibious assault ship to the Middle East, a U.S. official said Friday, while
Iran continued to launch widespread missile and drone attacks on Israel and neighbouring Gulf states.

items.title: Ukraine, its allies object to U.S. move to exempt Russian oil from sanctions for at least 30 days
items.summary_auto: The United States issued a 30-day licence for countries to buy Russian oil and petroleum products currently stranded ‌at sea in what Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said was a step to stabilize global energy markets roiled by the Iran war.

items.title: Hospital ERs in Canada are at a breaking point and have been for some time. Is this the new normal?
North Bay, Ont., tenant says he’s worse off after accepting landlord’s $40K offer to move out
items.summary_auto: After a months-long fight with his landlord, Shannon Lucas accepted a $40,000 payout to move out of the North Bay, Ont., townhouse where he lived for eight years, but says he would give it all back if he could stay.

items.title: Nova Scotia doctor's invention featured in hit medical drama The Pitt
items.summary_auto: The Ring Rescue appeared in the latest episode of the HBO series after one of the device's inventors, Dr. Kevin Spencer, met the show's lead actor, Noah Wyle, and producers at a medical trade show last year.

items.title: Dartmouth doctor’s invention featured in hit medical drama The Pitt
items.summary_auto: The Ring Rescue appeared in the latest episode of the HBO series after one of the device's inventors, Dr. Kevin Spencer, met the show's lead actor, Noah Wyle, and producers at a medical trade show last year.

items.title: How rare is it for a prime minister to attract 4 floor-crossers in 4 months?
items.summary_auto: Floor-crossing has always been a feature of Canadian politics dating back to the first Parliament — and seeing four opposition MPs jump ship to join the government in a matter of months is rare but not unprecedented.

items.title: Amid an energy crisis, the world is drawing on its oil reserves. Why doesn't Canada have any?
items.summary_auto: Because Canada is a net exporter of oil, it doesn't need to keep reserves. But some say that rule is outdated, and our at-capacity oil industry can't do much to help fill the gap created by stalled tanker traffic in the Strait of Hormuz.

items.title: Canada's hospital emergency rooms have hit a breaking point. Is it the new normal?
items.summary_auto: Hospital emergency rooms are overflowing, resulting in patients being treated in storage rooms and hallways as they wait days for a bed. Doctors say it's happening against a backdrop of behind-the-scenes backlogs, access issues and an aging population that are all impacting the flow of patients through the health-care system.

items.title: Poilievre set to unveil Conservative auto pact back in Canada's car capital Sunday
items.summary_auto: Canada's Opposition leader says his new plan would take GST off Canadian-made cars and eliminate tariffs to sell them in the U.S.

items.title: U.S. pressure on Venezuela hasn't eased, even as world's attention turns to Iran
items.summary_auto: Venezuela's acting president, Delcy Rodríguez, is trying to manage U.S. demands as she looks to hang on to the support of the Trump administration more than two months after it toppled Nicolás Maduro. It's a prospect that's being made more difficult because the U.S. is now at war with Iran.

items.title: 'Cascading effects' of Strait of Hormuz blockage getting worse by the day, energy and trade experts say
items.summary_auto: Rising oil prices are just the start. By blocking the Strait of Hormuz, global supply chains are at risk. For every day the war drags on, the list of product shortages grows. Shortages of fertilizer for farmers, critical minerals, and helium for semiconductor production are already being felt around the world.
